coredata.dc:title: Unveiling molecular alignment, dielectric and electrical conductivity of an unaligned 4-octyl-4′-cyanobiphenyl liquid crystal doped with carbon dots

coredata.dc:description: We report the achievement of vertical alignment in the smectic A (SmA) and nematic (N) phases of a 4-octyl-4′-cyanobiphenyl (8CB) LC doped with carbon dots (CDs, ∼2.8±0.72 nm) at concentrations ≤ 0.3 wt%. The composites were filled in indium tin oxide (ITO) sample cells without alignment layer (i.e., ITOWAL cells) and characterized using polarizing optical microscope and dielectric spectroscopic techniques. Optical textures displayed the attainment of induced vertical alignment (dark optical texture) even for the lowest concentration of 0.03 wt% used in the experiments. The enhanced dark state was observed with increasing concentration of up to 0.3 wt%. This was also verified by measuring the real dielectric permittivity (εʹ) of the composites. For instance, at 25ºC the value of εʹ for 0.03 wt% composite was 12.6 which increased to 14.8 for 0.3 wt% composite demonstrating the better vertical alignment. The appearance of short axis molecular relaxation in the dielectric loss (ε ̋) of all composites was another confirmation of induced vertical alignment. Measurements at various temperatures show that the induced vertical alignment remains stable throughout the SmA and N phases of 8CB. Additionally, bias voltage studies were conducted to assess the implication of an aligning voltage on εʹ and ε ̋. Electrical conductivity was calculated from ε ̋ and for the highest concentration, an increase of two orders of magnitude in the dc conductivity (σdc) compared to a polyimide alignment layer vertically aligned pure 8CB sample. The conductivity exhibited Arrhenius behavior for all composites. This work demonstrates that CDs induced vertical alignment in both SmA, and N phases could replace the conventional alignment techniques required to obtain the vertical alignment of LC materials. Moreover, these composites could pave the way forward for the fabrication of sensors and other optical devices due to their thermally stable alignment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image and (b) particle size distribution of CDs. The scale bar is 50 nm.

Optical textures of Sm A phase (T = 25°C) of (a) 0.00 wt%, (b) 0.03 wt%, (c) 0.05 wt%, (d) 0.1 wt%, and (e) 0.3 wt% CDs-8CB composites. ITOWAL sample cells of thickness 4 μm is used. P and A indicate polarizer and analyzer, respectively. The scale bar is 100 μm. Note that there are some very small defects in these structures, but overall excellent alignment.

Optical textures of N phase (T=35°C) (a) 0.00 wt%, (b) 0.03 wt%, (c) 0.05 wt%, (d) 0.1 wt% and (e) 0.3 wt% CDs-8CB composites. The ITOWAL sample cells of thickness 4 μm is used. P and A indicate polarizer and analyzer, respectively. The scale bar is 100 μm.
Temperature dependent optical texture of induced vertical alignment in CDs-8CB composites. P and A indicate polarizer and analyzer, respectively. The scale bar is 100 μm. The cell thickness is 4 μm.
Frequency dependent relative real part of the dielectric permittivity (εʹ) at (a) 25°C (SmA) and (b) 35°C (N) of CDs-8CB composites. No external DC bias voltage is applied.
Frequency dependent imaginary part of the dielectric permittivity (ε″) at (a) 25°C (SmA) and (b) 35°C (N) of CDs-8CB composites.
Temperature dependent εʹ of CDs-8CB composites at a constant frequency of 10 kHz.
Voltage dependent εʹ at (a) 25°C (SmA) and (b) 35°C (N) of CDs-8CB composites. The external bias of up to 20 V and 10 V is applied in SmA and N phases, respectively.

Angular frequency (ω) dependent conductivity (σ) of pure 8CB and CDs-8CB composites in the (a) SmA (25°C) and (b) N (35°C) phases, and fits using Jonscher’s Power Law (JPL). Symbols denote experimental data and solid lines represent the fitted curves.

Fitted Arrhenius plot illustrating the variation of logarithmic dc conductivity with the reciprocal of absolute temperature for pure 8CB and CDs-8CB composites. The R2 values range from 94 % to 99 % indicating the goodness of fit. Experimental data points are denoted by symbols and the solid lines depict the fitting achieved with Arrhenius equation.
Concentration dependent thermal activation energy (Ea) with error bar in (a) SmA and (b) N phases of pure 8CB and CDs-8CB composites.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the achievement of vertical alignment in the smectic A (SmA) and nematic (N) phases of a 4-octyl-4′-cyanobiphenyl (8CB) LC doped with carbon dots (CDs, ∼2.8±0.72 nm) at concentrations ≤ 0.3 wt%. The composites were filled in indium tin oxide (ITO) sample cells without alignment layer (i.e., ITOWAL cells) and characterized using polarizing optical microscope and dielectric spectroscopic techniques. Optical textures displayed the attainment of induced vertical alignment (dark optical texture) even for the lowest concentration of 0.03 wt% used in the experiments. The enhanced dark state was observed with increasing concentration of up to 0.3 wt%. This was also verified by measuring the real dielectric permittivity (εʹ) of the composites. For instance, at 25ºC the value of εʹ for 0.03 wt% composite was 12.6 which increased to 14.8 for 0.3 wt% composite demonstrating the better vertical alignment. The appearance of short axis molecular relaxation in the dielectric loss (ε ̋) of all composites was another confirmation of induced vertical alignment. Measurements at various temperatures show that the induced vertical alignment remains stable throughout the SmA and N phases of 8CB. Additionally, bias voltage studies were conducted to assess the implication of an aligning voltage on εʹ and ε ̋. Electrical conductivity was calculated from ε ̋ and for the highest concentration, an increase of two orders of magnitude in the dc conductivity (σdc) compared to a polyimide alignment layer vertically aligned pure 8CB sample. The conductivity exhibited Arrhenius behavior for all composites. This work demonstrates that CDs induced vertical alignment in both SmA, and N phases could replace the conventional alignment techniques required to obtain the vertical alignment of LC materials. Moreover, these composites could pave the way forward for the fabrication of sensors and other optical devices due to their thermally stable alignment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Universal familiarity and everyday experiences with technologies have made liquid crystal (LC) an augmenting field of research. Exhibiting peculiar properties of both crystalline solids and isotropic liquids such as anisotropy, ordering of molecules, fluidity, etc. make LCs a promoter of avant-garde technology [1]. Nematic liquid crystals (NLCs) possess the least ordering of LCs and exhibit molecules aligned preferentially along a common direction, called director (n) [2]. Their brisk response to external inputs make them one of the most exploited phases in the field of LCs. In the SmA phase, the molecules are arranged in layers and within the layer they have orientational ordering as in nematic phase but no positional ordering [3]. LCs has been used widely in numerous applications that ranges from display devices [4], phase modulators [5], optical switches [6], smart windows [7], sensors [8], tunable filters [9], photonic devices [10] etc. In order to enhance the user experience, LCs are also integrated in virtual and augmented reality which are ruling the market currently [11,12]. The basic requirement for the use of LC in the majority of these applications depends upon their ability to obtain excellent molecular alignment. There are mainly two types of LC alignment i.e., planar (n is parallel to the substrates) and vertical (n is perpendicular to the substrates) depending on the sign of dielectric anisotropy (Δε>0 or Δε<0) of LC materials [13,14]. Conventional alignment techniques have many issues, and new pathways are being explored for obtaining a uniform and defect free alignment that is cost effective [15]. In this quest, several techniques surfaced that may be promising alternatives to the traditional rubbing technique for instance oblique evaporation [16,17], deposition of surfactants [18], photoalignment [19], alignment using electric/magnetic field [20,21] and dispersion of nanoparticles [22–26].

The discovery of nanoparticles (NPs) remains as a significant pillar in the history of research owing to their salient properties and consequential impact across diverse fields of science and technology [27,28]. Therefore, doping/dispersing of NPs in LCs has been studied due to the profound effect NPs produce in the host LC materials [29–36]. Researchers have reported the change in optical, dielectric, electro-optical, thermal, electrical and other physical properties of LC when doped with various types of NPs [37–40]. Since last two to three decades, quantum dots (QDs) among NPs have remained a focal point of scholarly research because of their small size (2–10 nm, diameter), quantum confinement effect, tunable optical properties, and high quantum yield that eventually enables their utilization in state-of-the-art technologies [41–43]. Consequently, these properties eventually attracted the researchers around the globe to use QDs as dopants to LC materials (planar or vertically aligned) to achieve the significant improvement in the physical properties of LC materials required for next generation LC based displays and other photonic devices [44–48]. Here, we review some of the works in which QDs are doped in positive dielectric anisotropic (Δε>0) LCs and then filled into unaligned cells (i.e., without alignment layers and rubbing). For instance, Hegmann and co-workers reported that doping of hexadecylamine-capped CdSe QDs (varying size ∼ 2.5–5.2 nm) in phenyl pyrimidine-based LC (Felix-2900–03) induces vertical alignment when filled in a plain, untreated glass cells (microscopy glass slides and cover slip) at concentrations of 2 and 1 wt%, however thioglycolic acid-capped CdTe QDs displayed vertical alignment for concentration above 2 wt% [49]. A reduction in threshold voltage (Vth) and splay elastic constant (K11) were also reported for both QDs-LC composites. In a similar work, they utilized three different QDs (namely, QD1, QD2, QD3) as dopants in the same LC (Felix-2900–03) and found that QD3 (Zn doped CdSe QD) shows areas of vertical alignment with birefringent stripes in plain glass cells for a concentration of 5 wt% [50]. However, due to toxicity and related health issues of cadmium based QDs, researchers have focused on the synthesis and utilization of eco-friendly QDs in the past few years [51–54]. Few examples of eco-friendly QDs are carbon dots, indium phosphide (InP), Gallium phosphide (GaP), zinc oxide (ZnO), zinc sulfide (ZnS), CIS-ZnS, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), etc. [55–59]. Surprisingly, only limited reports are available on the doping of eco-friendly QDs in positive dielectric LC material filled in unaligned (i.e., without rubbing) sample cells. For instance, Lee et al. [24] demonstrated the formation of planar alignment in 5CB, NLC when doped with 1-D chain like clusters of CIS-ZnS QDs (at concentrations of 0.01 & 0.02 mol L−1) under the capillary action in an unaligned sample cell. The authors reported a superior electro-optic response in terms of reduction in Vth and response time of CIS-ZnS QDs-5CB composites. Therefore, it would be worth exploring the effect of doping of eco-friendly QDs on the molecular alignment, dielectric and electrical properties of positive dielectric LCs filled into unaligned sample cells.

It is worth pointing out here that among eco-friendly QDs, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered wide interest because of their eco-friendliness, biocompatibility, high stability in common solvents, fluorescence, high conductivity, etc. [60–62]. Moreover, they are the most economical and easy to prepare as compared to the other metal NPs. To exploit these features in the field of LCs, studies have been conducted to understand the effect of CDs on the molecular alignment, dielectric, electrical, electro-optical and non-linear optical properties of LC (nematic and SmA) materials filled into planar aligned sample cells [63–71]. On the other hand, reports are rare in case of unaligned sample cells. To the best of our knowledge there is only one report wherein the impact of CDs on the molecular alignment, dielectric and electrical properties of 5CB, NLC infiltrated into sample cells of ITO without alignment layer (ITOWAL) has been investigated [72]. However, currently there appears to be no report on the exploration of doping CDs on the molecular alignment, dielectric and electrical conductivity of LC materials possessing both nematic (N) and SmA phases filled in ITOWAL cells. Here, we report the effect of CDs on the optical texture (i.e., molecular alignment), dielectric, and electrical properties of 4-octyl-4′-cyanobiphenyl (8CB) LC filled in ITOWAL cells of thickness 4 μm. Crossed polarized optical microscope and frequency-dependent dielectric spectroscopic techniques have been used to understand the molecular alignment, dielectric parameters and electrical conductivity of CDs-8CB composites under the variation of temperature, frequency, and dopant concentration. Importantly, our results clearly demonstrate that thermally stable vertical alignment can be induced in both SmA and N phases of 8CB in ITOWAL cells even at the lowest concentration (i.e., 0.03 wt%) of dopant CDs. Dopant concentrations lower than 0.03 wt% were not studied as the induced vertical alignment at 0.03 wt% did not have good optical contrast. Moreover, dielectric permittivity and electrical conductivity of 8CB are also tuned by the dopant CDs. We anticipate the use of such nanocomposites in low-cost LC based optical devices such as optical sensors and other photonic devices.

The LC used in this work is 4-octyl-4′-cyanobiphenyl (8CB) purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used in its original state without any refinement. The phase sequence of 8CB is Cr (21.5 °C) SmA (32.5 °C) N (40.5°C) I, where Cr, SmA, N and I denote Crystal, Smectic A, Nematic and Isotropic phases, respectively. The dopant material Carbon dots (CDs) was prepared using one pot selective synthesis method [73] by the Kumar group as reported earlier [74]. Moreover, Minervini et al. [75] reported the structural details of these CDs. They mentioned that carboxyl, hydroxyl, functionalized and non-functionalized alkyl groups are attached to the periphery of CDs. The diameter of the CDs was confirmed through HRTEM and was found to be 2.8±0.72 nm [76]. The mixtures of CDs and LC were made by precisely weighing the required quantities of LC using a microbalance and adding appropriate amounts of CDs in chloroform. A stock dispersion of CDs (0.1 w/v%) was made and desired concentrations (0.03, 0.05, 0.1 and 0.3 wt%) were combined with LC in vials which were ultrasonicated for about 2 hours to achieve a homogenous dispersion. A point to be noted here is that in our experiments, we refer 0.03 wt% and 0.3 wt% as lowest and highest concentrations, respectively. The prepared composites underwent thermal treatment in an oven to facilitate evaporation of the solvent from the composite matrices. Evaporation was at a temperature surpassing the boiling point of chloroform (61.2ºC) and sustained at that temperature for about an hour. Thereafter, the composites were filled in an unaligned (no rubbed Polyimide) ITO coated 4 μm thick sample cell (Instec Inc., USA). In order to compare the molecular alignment, dielectric and electrical conductivity of composites, pure 8CB was also filled in a homeotropically aligned 9 μm thick sample cell procured from Instec Inc., USA.

Optical texture and dielectric studies were conducted in the prepared composites. Polarized optical microscope (CENSICO International 13809, India) in conjunction with a camera (AmScope, FMA050) was employed to record optical textures. For dielectric measurements, an LCR meter (nF, ZM2376, Japan) with a frequency range of 10 Hz to 5.5 MHz was used. The temperature of the sample cells was maintained using a customized hot stage linked with a circulating water bath (Thermotech, AQS-WB-200, India) having an accuracy of ± 0.5°C. The AC voltage for dielectric measurements was kept to 300 mV. The morphological study of the CDs was conducted using high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Tecnai G20, USA) operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.

Fig. 1 (a) depicts the HRTEM image of well dispersed CDs. The average particle size distribution (2.8±0.72 nm) fitted with gaussian function has been shown in Fig. 1 (b). The particle size was analyzed using ImageJ software and more than 60 N particles were considered for the estimation. The morphological aspects of CDs can be visualized through this high-resolution image of the CDs whereby the CDs are observed to be mostly quasi spherical in shape.

Fig. 2 demonstrates the optical texture of SmA phase of pure 8CB and CDs-8CB composites in the SmA phase at constant temperature (T=25°C) viewed through crossed polarizers in an optical polarizing microscope. The typical focal conic texture shown by pure 8CB (Fig. 2 a) eventually confirms that the ITOWAL sample cell could not align the SmA phase as expected. Interestingly, the doping with CDs produces vertical alignment in SmA phase even at the concentration of 0.03 wt% (lowest concentration among four different concentrations used in our experiments) as observed in Fig. 2 (b). Moreover, the further increase of the dopant concentration produces more uniform vertical alignment (Figs. 2 c and 2 d) and for the highest concentration of 0.3 wt%, the optical texture becomes intensely dark (Fig. 2 e). The presence of dark texture remains almost invariant with respect to the rotation of the sample cell fixed on the rotational stage of polarising optical microscope eventually confirms the induced vertical alignment. A point to be noted here is that the absence of alignment layer eventually facilitates the interaction of guest particles (CDs, here) directly with the ITO layer. The inclination of CDs to accumulate at the substrate surface provides sufficient force to reorient the molecules vertically in the absence of any alignment layer.

The stability of induced vertical alignment in SmA phase was then examined in N phase (T = 35°C) of composites filled into the same ITOWAL sample cells. The Schlieren texture with two and four brush disclinations ( Fig. 3 a) is observed in N phase of pure 8CB sample as expected. However, induced vertical alignment in N phase of CDs-8CB composites is seen (Fig. 3 b,c,d&e). The quality of induced alignment is strongly dependent on the concentration of CDs. The darkness of dark state in N phase is found to increase with increase in the dopant concentration as observed in SmA phase. The induced vertical alignment is observed to be stable in the N phase. It doesn’t exhibit any degradation in the texture as it transitioned from SmA to N phase. Once more, this can be attributed to the interaction of CDs with the ITO surface which remains stable even through the change of phase. Surprisingly, the ability to reorient the director to a vertical state works well even when the quantity of CDs added into host 8CB, LC is the smallest (0.03 wt%). This demonstrates the strong influence of CDs in the induction of vertical alignment even in the absence of an alignment layer. Optical micrographs of vertically aligned sample cells filled with pure 8CB are shown in Figure S1. It appears that the quality of induced vertical alignment in SmA and N phases of 0.3 wt% CDs-8CB composite is better than the vertical alignment of pure 8CB in a homeotropically anchored sample cell.

The thermal stability of such induced vertical alignment was also investigated. It was observed that all prepared composites exhibited highly stable alignment with an increase in temperature as shown in Fig. 4. The variation in temperature didn’t degrade the dark texture and samples showed negligible alignment change as the composite traversed from the SmA to N phase. Fig. 4 clearly demonstrates the alignment remains stable till TNI for all composites. However, 0.05 and 0.3 wt% composites show a decrease in TNI by 1ºC.
Dielectric spectroscopic techniques allow one to get a deeper understanding of the molecular dynamics exhibited in these systems [77–80].

Fig. 5 depicts the frequency dependent variation of the real part of the dielectric permittivity (εʹ) with the doping of CDs in both the SmA and N phases of 8CB, LC. For frequency <100 Hz an increased value of εʹ with decreasing frequency is attributed to the ionic relaxation in the LC medium [69,81,82]. For higher frequencies, above about 200 Hz, εʹ remains constant with increasing frequency until 50 kHz and then shows a decreasing trend at higher frequencies. In the SmA phase (Fig. 5 a), the value of εʹ in the plateau region for the 0.03 wt% composite obtained its lowest value (12.6 @ 10 kHz) out of all composites under investigation. An increase in the concentration of CDs to 0.05, 0.1 and 0.3 wt% shows an increase in εʹ reaching its maximum value for 0.3 wt% (14.8 @10 kHz). All composites showed vertical alignment in optical texture on a macroscopic scale (Fig. 2). The ability of CDs to align the SmA phase vertically without the use of any external factors (anchoring force by rubbed polyimide, electric field, etc.), especially at such low concentrations makes it an alternative to the traditional aligning methods. A similar trend was also observed in N phase as shown in Fig. 5 (b), where once more increasing concentration of CDs exhibited increased permittivity. Here, the 0.3 wt% composite had the highest value for εʹ at 14.8 and 0.03 wt% composite showed the smallest value of 12.6 at 10 kHz frequency. The composites with intermediate concentrations of 0.05 and 0.1 wt% almost had an overlapping value of εʹ in N phase. The value of εʹin CDs-8CB composites can be compared with the εʹ shown by the vertically aligned cell in Figure S2. Interestingly, the 0.3 wt% composite shows a value of εʹ of 14.8 that is even higher than the vertically aligned pure 8CB value of 13. This indicates that quality of vertical alignment of composite is better than the conventional vertical alignment.

The development of such induced vertical alignment was further investigated by recording the imaginary part of the dielectric permittivity (ε″) of CDs-8CB composites as shown in Fig. 6. The appearance of short axis molecular relaxation for all composites yet again confirmed vertical alignment [24,77,83]. In the SmA phase, the short axis molecular relaxation peak for the 0.03 wt% composite was observed at 3.49×105 Hz. The peak shifted towards the lower frequency side with increasing concentration of CDs, where for 0.3 wt% composite, relaxation peak was ∼ 17 % lower (i.e. 2.9×105 Hz) (Fig. 6 a). The short axis molecular relaxation peak in the N phase also produced a similar trend whereby 0.03 wt% composite had its peak at 3.51×105 Hz and 0.3 wt% composite exhibited at 3.14×105 Hz (Fig. 6 b). The short axis molecular relaxation peak is also compared with pure 8CB in homeotropic cells where the relaxation peak in the SmA phase is ∼ 2.47×105 Hz and for the N ∼2.51×105 Hz (Figure S3). Additionally, Cole-Cole plot for the short axis molecular relaxation has been illustrated for all composites in Figure S4 which have a semicircular pattern that is in accordance with Debye process [77,78].

After examining the εʹ at one temperature in the SmA and N phases, the temperature dependent εʹ was also probed at a constant frequency of 10 kHz as shown in the Fig. 7. As seen, the vertical alignment induced by the CDs remains highly stable throughout the SmA and N phases of 8CB, LC. At T=25ºC, the value of εʹ for 0.03 wt% composite is 12.6 which increases to 13.0 and 13.4 for 0.05 and 0.1 wt%, respectively, and 0.3 wt% shows 14.8 the highest εʹ of all the composites studied. As seen in the graph, the value remains more or less monotonous with varying temperature and decreases as it reaches close to nematic to isotropic transition temperature (TNI). Composites of 0.05 and 0.3 wt% exhibit a reduced TNI of 1°C, however 0.03 & 0.1 wt% exhibit similar TNI to that of pure 8CB i.e. 40ºC which corroborates well with the temperature dependent optical textures discussed in reference to Fig. 4. The temperature dependent εʹ for pure 8CB is shown in Figure S5 where the TNI is observed to be at 40ºC. Moreover, the value of εʹ at T=25ºC is 13 lower than the value seen in 0.3 wt% composite is 14.8.

External bias voltages of up to 20 V and 10 V were applied in SmA and N phases, respectively and the variation of εʹ is displayed in Fig. 8. In the SmA phase, the value of εʹ remains constant up to approximately 14 V whereafter it increases until 20 V for all lower concentrations (0.03, 0.05, 0.1 wt%) as shown in Fig. 8 (a). However, for the 0.3 wt% composite, the value remains essentially constant indicating near perfect vertical alignment. Since the N phase (T = 35°C) is less ordered than the SmA, only voltages up to10 V were applied and necessary to achieve the saturated state as shown in Fig. 8 (b). The value of εʹ increases rapidly in N phase at the application of bias and reaches a saturation state by 10 V for lower concentrations (0.03, 0.05, 0.1 wt%). Similar to SmA phase, 0.3 wt% composite does not show much variation in εʹ with respect to the applied voltage in the N phase. It is worth pointing out here that the application of an external bias voltage bridges the gap that exists between attaining the perfect vertical state and the vertical state induced by CDs in lower concentration composites. Moreover, we have explored the effect of external bias on the frequency dependent ε″ and on the Cole-Cole plot of the composites which are demonstrated in Figure S6 and Figure S7, respectively. In the frequency dependent ε″ plots (Figure S6 (a)) it can be observed that the application of 20 V external bias in SmA phase has caused the overlapping of short axis relaxation peaks for all the composites except for 0.03 wt%. And in the N phase, application of 10 V exhibited a complete overlapping of peaks for all composites (Figure S6 (b)). The Cole-Cole plot (Figure S7) also illustrated that the application of external voltage produced a better and enlarged semicircle for all composites and this relaxation is found to be in accordance with Debye process.

The effect of CDs on the electrical conductivity of the 8CB, LC has also been closely scrutinized in this work. Since CDs exhibit an inherently high conductivity [61] doping them in 8CB is expected to impact the composites’ conductivity. The basic equation relating conductivity (σ) to dielectric loss (ε″) is given as:
(1) σ = 2πfεoε″

In this analysis, we take into account the three regions that are generally ascertained from a conductivity plot. Low frequency region (≤102 Hz) is accounted for by the electrode polarization effect. The intermediate region (102-104Hz) focuses on conductivity that is independent of frequency often denoted as dc conductivity. The high frequency (≥104 Hz) behavior which strongly depends on frequency is represented as ac conductivity [84]. Here, Fig. 9 demonstrates the dependency of total conductivity (σ) in response to varying angular frequency (ω). The intermediate and high frequency region obeys Jonscher’s Power law [81] represented as:
(2) σ(ω) = σdc + Aωn

Here σdc is the dc conductivity, A and n are the empirical parameters, wherein n generally lies in the range of 0<n<1. In the present work, the value of n for all samples under investigation was found to surpass the Jonscher’s limit [82]. At intermediate frequencies (102-104Hz), Aωn becomes really small so the total conductivity consists of only dc conductivity (σdc) [85]. Fig. 9 (a) demonstrates a gradual increase in conductivity with addition of CDs in SmA phase. From the fitted data σdc for pure 8CB was found to be 7.91×10−9 S/m which increased by one order of magnitude for 0.03,0.05 & 0.1 wt% composites. However, for 0.3 wt% composite it got enhanced by the two orders of magnitude with σdc =2.23×10−7 S/m. The rise in conductivity can be associated with the CDs ion releasing regime, leading to enhanced ion mobility at high concentrations. Additionally, the flow of ions is more pronounced since the alignment for all samples under analysis is vertical. In the N phase (Fig. 9 (b)) due to less orientational ordering and viscosity than SmA, σdc for pure 8CB was 3.69×10−8 S/m. A smaller viscosity leads to easier mobility of ions in the medium and subsequently higher values of conductivity. Composites manifested similar behavior in the SmA phase, wherein σdc increased up to a single order of magnitude for 0.03, 0.05 & 0.1 wt% composite, and a two-order magnitude for 0.3 wt% composite with a corresponding σdc value of 1.2×10−6 S/m. Surprisingly, in the low frequency region, the electrode polarization effect seems to be less interfering than in the planar aligned sample cells [71].

Fig. 10 illustrates the effect of temperature on the dc conductivity of pure 8CB and CDs-8CB composites. The dc conductivity follows the Arrhenius equation:
(3)

wherein, Ea denotes the activation energy, kB is the Boltzmann constant and σo indicates pre-exponential factor. In the SmA phase, an increase in conductivity is seen with rise in temperature. The increase in concentration also shows an augmented conductivity due to the ion releasing phenomenon exhibited by CDs as discussed in Fig. 9. Among all composites, the 0.3 wt% composite has the highest conductivity in both the SmA and N phases. It is illustrated that the value of conductivity increased by 4.6 times in pure 8CB over the temperature range studied and for 0.3 wt% it elevated by 5.4 times. The ions released from the CDs dissolve easily in 8CB due to the presence of the cyano group [86]. In addition to the ease of flow made possible by the vertical alignment, these dissolved ions find a more accessible route for their mobility since the N phase possess lower viscosity. All these factors together produce a higher level of conductivity with the addition of CDs in 8CB material.

Variation in the activation energy (Ea) with concentration is depicted in Fig. 11 for pure 8CB and CDs-8CB composites. In the SmA phase, Ea increases with increasing dopant concentration as shown in Fig. 11 (a) except for 0.1 wt% composite. This can be ascribed to the slowing down of ionic mobility with rise in temperature thus giving lower value of slope (Fig. 10). The lowest value for Ea (1.06 eV) is seen for pure 8CB. However, 0.3 wt% composite shows the highest value of Ea (1.55 eV). Higher ordering of the SmA phase poses a hurdle in the mobility of ions subsequently addition of CDs causes rise in Ea. However, in N phase, pure 8CB shows the highest value for Ea and for composites it decreases significantly (Fig. 11 b). The lower molecular ordering and attenuated viscosity of the N phase facilitates smoother flow of ions resulting in reduced Ea even with increasing CDs concentration.

In this work, we have reported the effect of doping of CDs (0.03, 0.05, 0.1 and 0.3 wt%) on the molecular alignment (optical texture), dielectric, and electrical features of 8CB composites filled in an ITOWAL sample cells. Polarized optical micrographs revealed the induction of vertical alignment in both SmA and N phases of 8CB by dopant CDs. An optically dark texture was observed to become more pronounced with increasing concentration and demonstrated a completely uniform dark texture at 0.3 wt%, the highest concentration studied. Dielectric spectroscopic studies also illustrated that 0.3 wt% composite had the highest value of εʹ, even though all samples exhibited vertical alignment. Temperature dependent optical texture and dielectric studies showed that the vertical alignment thus produced by CDs is thermally stable and doesn’t exhibit any form of degradation upon the phase transition from SmA to N phase. Electrical conductivity studies showed that addition of CDs increases the conductivity of the host 8CB material and produces an enhancement of two orders of magnitude in both the SmA and N phases for the highest concentration of CDs, 0.3 wt%. The increase in DC conductivity with temperature can also be analyzed through Arrhenius plots. The observed effect of CDs in 8CB, LC can be helpful in eliminating the expensive aligning layers needed to attain a vertical alignment in the fabrication of flexible display devices, optical shutters, sensors, and modulators. Moreover, addition of such QDs can be helpful in attaining not only good quality vertical alignment but also ones that are thermally stable.
